planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Bat Face Cuphea is grown as a tender perennial in zones 9–11 — it may survive mild zone 9 winters in sheltered locations, behaving as a perennial in warmer areas and a warm-season annual in colder areas of zone 9. Prune lightly in late winter to encourage fresh growth and abundant new flowering. In zone 9 it thrives in the hottest summer months and blooms continuously from spring through frost, becoming more floriferous as heat increases.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — cuphea is drought-tolerant once established and blooms better in lean soil than in rich, consistently moist conditions; Bat Face is a cuphea cultivar with one of the most distinctively shaped and visually memorable flowers in the ornamental plant world — each small, 0.5-inch tubular flower is deep crimson-red with two spreading, rounded purple petals at the tip that create an unmistakable bat face impression when viewed from the front, complete with the suggestion of eyes, nose, and ears in the petal arrangement. The bat face appearance is not accidental or fanciful — the distinctive petal structure is genuinely face-like in a way that is immediately recognized by most observers, making Bat Face cuphea the most conversation-starting small plant in the summer garden. The hummingbird-attractive tubular flower structure, combined with continuous bloom from spring through frost and excellent heat tolerance, makes Bat Face an outstanding zone 9 summer performer. The moderate spreading, bushy habit covers itself in hundreds of the tiny bat-face flowers simultaneously during peak bloom, creating an effect that is both charming and genuinely unusual in the summer garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cuphea for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Hummingbird Plants for Gulf Coast
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Perennials for Zone 9

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–April. Amistad is a large salvia reaching 4–5 feet tall in zone 9 — allow ample space in the back of borders. It blooms from late spring through frost without deadheading, producing successive flushes of deep violet-purple flowers that are reliably the most intensely colored purple-violet available among summer sages. In zone 9 it may survive as a short-lived perennial in sheltered locations, though it often behaves as an annual. Take cuttings in fall to overwinter indoors as insurance.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Amistad Salvia blooms best without excessive fertilization; Amistad (Spanish for 'friendship') is a salvia hybrid raised by the late Argentine plantsman Hector Cattan, believed to be a cross involving Salvia guaranitica and Salvia gesneriiflora, producing large (4–5 foot), upright, extremely floriferous plants with deep violet-purple flowers of exceptional saturation — one of the most intensely colored blue-purple flowers available among summer-blooming plants of any kind. The tubular violet flowers with contrasting dark calyces are a premier hummingbird and swallowtail butterfly nectar source that attracts pollinators from significant distances. The color is genuinely impressive: a pure, saturated, gem-like violet that maintains its intensity through summer heat without fading, providing a bold, reliable color presence in the back of sunny borders from late spring through frost. Zone 9 gardeners in the Gulf South have embraced Amistad as one of the best large warm-season salvias available — its combination of size, intense color, heat tolerance, and continuous bloom fills the critical role of a dominant back-of-border plant through the entire summer season.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Salvia Amistad Plant of the Year
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Salvia Species for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Salvias for Zone 9

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Husker's Red is among the most heat-tolerant penstemon species but may decline after 2–3 years in zone 9's heat and humidity — treat as a short-lived perennial and divide or replant from seed periodically. Plants bloom in late spring (May–June) and go semi-dormant in summer heat; the dramatic red foliage provides interest throughout the growing year even when not in bloom. In zone 9 light afternoon shade improves persistence and extends the bloom season.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil — penstemon demands excellent drainage and fails in consistently moist or heavy soils; Husker's Red is an All-America Selections award-winning cultivar of Foxglove Penstemon (Penstemon digitalis), a native prairie and woodland edge perennial, selected for its dramatic burgundy-red to maroon foliage that provides striking ornamental value throughout the growing season even when the plant is not in flower. The foliage color is the plant's primary landscape feature — the rich, wine-red rosette of leaves in spring and early summer, the upright dark stems, and the red-tinted bracts on the flower stalks create a dramatic plant that reads as deeply colored even before the pale lavender-white, foxglove-like tubular flowers appear. The spring flower display (May–June) adds a secondary ornamental dimension: tall, airy spikes of white to pale lavender tubular flowers on 2–3 foot stems attracting bumblebees and hummingbirds. As a native North American prairie species, Penstemon digitalis is adapted to the range of conditions typical of the Eastern US, including the heat and humidity of the Gulf South.
sources: All-America Selections — Penstemon Husker's Red Award
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Penstemon digitalis Native Plant
North Carolina State University Extension — Penstemon for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Becky is more heat-tolerant than most Shasta daisy cultivars but still performs as a short-lived perennial in zone 9's heat and humidity — expect to divide and replant every 2–3 years for continued vigor. Deadhead spent blooms to extend the flowering season from June–August in zone 9. The tall, upright stems (30–36 inches) produce cut flowers that last 7–10 days in a vase. In zone 9 plants bloom June–August; provide afternoon shade to reduce heat stress during the peak summer bloom period.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Shasta daisies must have excellent drainage and fail in wet, heavy soils; Becky is the most heat-tolerant Shasta daisy cultivar widely available, developed for performance in the hot summers of the American South and named for Becky Stewart, a Georgia gardener who selected the heat-tolerant seedling. The classic single white daisy flowers with golden-yellow central discs on long, sturdy, non-flopping stems represent the archetypal daisy form — clean, cheerful, and versatile in garden designs and cut flower arrangements. Becky's advantage over other Shasta daisies is precisely its ability to produce good quality cut flowers and garden display in zone 8–9 conditions that collapse most other varieties — the upright, non-flopping stems (critical for a quality cut flower), combined with extended blooming and heat persistence, make Becky the definitive Shasta daisy for Southern gardens. The white flowers work with virtually any color palette and any garden style, from formal to cottage to naturalistic, making Becky one of the most broadly useful perennial flowers for zone 9 garden designers.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Shasta Daisy Becky Selection
University of Georgia Extension — Leucanthemum Becky for Southern Gardens
Mississippi State University Extension — Heat-Tolerant Perennial Daisies

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–June. Red Shiso self-seeds prolifically — one or two plants in the first year will result in dozens of volunteers in subsequent years from dropped seeds. Allow a few plants to go to seed in desirable locations; otherwise harvest all flowering stalks before seeds form to prevent unwanted spread. In zone 9 red shiso grows rapidly in summer heat and thrives in conditions that would stress most cool-season herbs. Partial shade is tolerated and may produce larger, more tender leaves.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Red Shiso (Perilla frutescens var. purpurea) is the red-purple-leaved form of Japanese shiso — a herb central to Japanese and Korean cuisine with a distinctive flavor combining anise, mint, basil, and cinnamon in a complex, aromatic profile unlike any other commonly grown herb. The deep red-purple to burgundy leaves are used fresh in Japanese food preparations: wrapping nigiri sushi and maki rolls, as a garnish for sashimi, mixed into rice, and used in the traditional preparation of umeboshi (Japanese pickled plums) where red shiso provides the characteristic color and flavor of the classic pink umeboshi. Red shiso produces the vivid red pigment (anthocyanin perillins) that colors umeboshi and red shiso juice — the brilliant magenta-red natural dye is water-soluble and stable enough for food and beverage coloring. Beyond Japanese and Korean applications, red shiso's ornamental burgundy foliage makes it valuable in kitchen garden designs where its striking color provides contrast against green vegetables, and the self-seeding habit provides a reliable annual return once established.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Herbs for Florida Gardens
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Shiso Varieties for Hawaii and Warm Climates
USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System — Perilla frutescens
